What about side effects? What about side effects? Side effects would have to go around how it works. Side effects would have to go around how it works. How long before it works? Let's keep going for a point of demonstration. Okay, we're switching over to the second shortest person in the room. Doug, you're relieved of your news. Jose, girl. I'm calling the news. I do have a voice. Okay, keep calling it out. Alright, so we have Work and I Get song, Medicare. Does Medicare cover it? Am I too old? Do I have to eat it? How much? Is an aging natural? What age do I have to be who uses it? If you guys want to call out deletions, just call out deletions too. Alright, so I'm going to start from the top. Is it covered by Medicare? So for demonstration purposes, let's just stop there. You guys see how this is going, right? Okay, next thing. Doug, you're back on, buddy. Grab a sharky. Okay, we're starting at the top now. So here is where we're entering step number four. So again, just to recap, step number one is the pitch is the idea. Step number two is the brainstorming of objections. Step number three is the organizing of objections from top to bottom. Step number three is the organizing of objections from top to bottom in the order that we think we have to sell the reader when overcoming those objections. Step number four, which is where we are right now, which is creating mini-subheads that will begin the sales process of overcoming that objective. So think of your mini-subheads as mini-headlines. These should all have stopping power. Every one of these should have stopping power, meaning that every one of these on their own should almost be like alternative headlines that you could use for your promotion. That's how powerful these subheads need to be. So read us the first objection up there. Why haven't I heard about this before? Why haven't I heard about this before? You take that away as an individual copywriter and begin doing what we're doing now. So this is where you disconnect from the group and go back on your own. Does that make sense? So step number five, right, is that now you have all of these things after you enter, exit step number four, you have all of these things in a Word document that you now understand is the sales flow of your entire promo. So this is the same equivalent to them going out in Hollywood, pulling out the cameras, bringing in the actors and beginning to shoot the scenes because now the scenes are all in order. Does everybody make sense for that? Any questions on that? Okay. So this is the point that you're going to begin to put in the work. This is, think about this as the start of Hell Week at this point. So you have the next 30 days to do everything that we're now outlining right here. Assuming your idea got green like yesterday. If it didn't get green like you got to go back to the drawing board and find your idea first, right? But this is what you have the next 30 days to do. When you get into Hell Week, you enter into step number five. Step number five, and I'll give you a tip on this in just a second, is where you're going to begin adding the actual words, the proof that go in between these different subheads. But this should be easy because you know, for instance, during the who are you section, what belongs in that subhead? What belongs in between? That's it. So you know you have to fill out a subhead in between this subhead and that subhead that you have to prove authority. You have to sell authority. You have to sell Brad Lemmon. You have to sell his trustworthy nest. You have to sell his credentials. You have to sell that they should believe him as a person. So this keeps you on track, right? So here comes a real quick little tip. And I didn't know this until Nick Chalker showed me something the other day. Matt and I are writing a Bond promo that we just did this for the other day. And we laid this whole thing out, and Matt and I are going to co-write it in Google Docs. So Matt puts the entire thing in Google Docs, and then I was talking to Nick one day, and I said, Nick, I'll show this Google Doc with you so you can follow along with how Matt and I are doing it so you can see how this process is shaping up. So Nick writes to me, and he goes, how did you create that outline in Google Docs? And I'm like, I have no clue what you're saying. And he's like, there's an outline in Google Docs. I'm like, I definitely do not see an outline in my Google Docs. So I'm like, come up here. So he runs up here, and I reload the Google Doc on my screen, and there is no outline. And I'm like, I don't know. I think Nick's crazy at this point. Nick's like, no, I'm telling you, there's an outline. He's like, log out and log back in. So I log out, and I log back in, and sure enough, there is an outline in Google Docs that I never knew was there. It can help you tremendously now that I've learned the secret. So here's the quick little secret to how you do this. If you start up a Google Doc, and you write all of your subheads, on the left-hand side of Google Docs, there's something that says, change from normal text to subheaded text. When you change that, an outline should appear on the left-hand side of that document. It's pretty awesome, because now, on the left-hand side of your document, you will see a running outline of your entire promotion, no matter where you are in filling out your subheads. If you fill out your subheads in the main document, you can always see your subheads on the left-hand side. So you always see what is going to be the next subhead. It's pretty awesome. It's going to change the way I write, because I've never seen that outline before. Okay, so now I'm coming back to step number five real quick. So step number five, again, what you're going to have to do is you're going to have to fill in in between your subheads. But this should be relatively easy, because you know what cell you have to make in that subheaded section. So, like Sonya was saying, in the Who Are You section, in between this subhead and this subhead, your only cell that you have to make is one Brad Lemoy. Pretty easy, right? So you know here, in this subheaded section, why tell me that you're going to have to sell the idea that he finds it some reason to share this information? There's some benefit to him to share this information. Maybe he is on that personal mission, because maybe one of his family members died from one of these age-related diseases, and he never wants to see another American go through the suffering that his family members saw. That's what you need to do in that subhead. So this is keeping you on track and organized. Again, to come full circle back to this copyboarding thing, when I see CopyDig, it gets turned into me, and it's like 95 pages. It's all over the fucking place, right? It's all over the fucking place. Like someone is proving Brad Lemoy up here, and then reproving Brad Lemoy down here, and then reproving Brad Lemoy in a much bigger way down here. And I'm like, this is all fucked up, right? Like that's where you're going to have to delete 15 pages, because it's like you don't need to prove Brad Lemoy down here. If the person's got down here, they already believed Brad Lemoy, or else they wouldn't have still been in the promo. If there was still an objection about who the fuck are you on page 55, like they're not going to be reading, because they would have felt frustrated by that and not believe that person who had exited the promotion. If anything just destroys your credibility too, it's why, I'm just thinking, well why is he keep telling about himself when I already believed him? I have literally seen promos that introduce the author three times without acknowledging that the author has been introduced two other times. That seems fucking crazy to me. Like the guy's got schizophrenia or something. You know, like it'll say like, hi, I'm Brad Lemoy up here, I'm not joking you. And then it'll be like on page 40 that'll say, hi, my name is Brad Lemoy. And then on page like 65 it'll say, my name, by the way, is Brad Lemoy. And you're like, who the fuck are you? This fucking guy's crazy. I'm not even a Virgin Mary's milk this old from this point. So, again, that's like an egregious example, but it's happening. I'm telling you it happens. And it happens because the writer is going, I'm going to write brilliantly from the top of my head. And they don't even fucking realize that they did it two other times in the fucking promo. And that shit will happen again and again. You'll see the same lines about proof. You know, uh oh. I feel like that's the copyboard of God coming down. What's that? Uh, Doug. I can't get that thing. Whoa. Um. Okay. All making sense so far. Hey Joe, just to back up on what you said, if you go into the document, if you go up in the chapter, if you go into tools, it specifically says document outline. And the only thing that will show up is anything that's in some sort of a subhead format. And I did not have my document outline turned on, which is why I felt Nick was crazy when he kept telling me there was an outline there, and I kept telling them that there wasn't. So I think it's a pretty awesome tool. Because again, on the left-hand side, you're going to be able to see all of your subheads. And on the right-hand side, you're going to see your entire full-length promotion. So you can know if you're in this section, you can see easily that that's the next place you're going that you need to overcome. Okay, so, um, let's talk about in between these subheads, right? This is where Ryan's formula is coming into play. Because if you use this in between each subhead, like Ryan was saying earlier, so how does it work? Can you, you're going to have to riff for a second. But if you look at how does it work, which is turning into blocks, aging proteins, you know that this is going to be a mechanism section. So you're just being acclaimed there, right? A blocks agent, you've got to like back that up with proof. So what are like three pieces of proof you guys can think of just based on the ones that I provided on that sheet. You know, one of them might be explaining how the mechanism works, you know, some sort of semi-logical argument. Another might be like a picture, a diagram. And another might be just a piece of social proof saying it works from somebody else. Yeah, and that social proof could be, you know, a case study, a piece of proof of someone actually doing this. It could be a scientific study. So now you've got your claim. Get the proof. And then the key thing you never want to forget is if you, what's in it for them, what's the benefit? Just saying it doesn't really give anything. The benefit might be because it can, it can block aging proteins. Therefore, it can cure just about any disease. Or therefore, it's not invasive unlike other forms of medication or surgery. So you always going to have that benefit. What's in it for them? Because it's telling them that claim and that proof's not enough. I mean, well, you got to tie it back to them personally and how it benefits you. Let's do the same thing just as another example on the who are you section. So the claim that you could make is I'm the guy that doctors turn to when they want to know what to do next. And that could be true around Brad because he was the ghost writer or I don't want to throw too much stuff out there. I mean, he did work with Dr. Andrew Wilde, who was a pretty famous doctor that's appeared on a lot of pretty national television shows. So he could say, I'm the guy that a famous doctor turned to when he wanted advice. Okay, so that's your claim, right? So your proof could be, you know, I've appeared on TV 32 times. I've written more than 200,000 words in all of these bestselling books. And it could be a testimonial from Dr. Andrew Wilde about how great Brad is. So there you got your three pieces of proof. And then your benefit could be, you know, never trust, right? Like I would say that you should never trust the mainstream medicine, this alternative medicine doctor. If you listen to what I have to say, you're going to learn alternative cures before anybody else will ever tell you because they just don't know. So that kind of objection, claim, proof benefit can be used for each one of these subheaded sections. So let's talk about questions. Does everybody see generally how this works? Does everyone see generally how to apply it? So you have your idea that you pitched yesterday. We talked about finding pieces of proof. We talked about the idea of how powerful a mechanism can be, you know, with the influence techniques and the authoritative pieces of proof. So again, you're still sharpening the ax here before you ever begin chopping down that tree. You continue to sharpen that ax by now saying, I have my idea. I have my proof points. You know, I have my authoritative proof that I want to make. I know I could find these things in case studies as it relates to Jim's idea with medical journals and things. This is going to be the next step of the process, organizing these mini-cells in a way that makes sense for the reader. This should be done in the next month. So this is what you're spending the next month of your life on. Again, the only side note to that is that if you didn't have an idea that got green-lit by somebody, go find an idea quickly. And then repitching your ideas. You have 30 ideas in that idea sheet. Pitch your second-best idea to people. Try to get that idea some feedback. Once you do have those ideas, this is the next 30 days of your life. Take your copy buddy. Say, let's do this thing. Let's organize these post-it notes. Let's write these sub-eds. Let me read these sub-eds back to you. Then when you get to Hell Week, all you're doing is filling in between your sub-eds, which should be relatively easy because you have all your proof. You know your idea. It's organized in a way. Now you just begin the easiest part of the process, which is what I believe to be the writing process. It's only easy because you've done all the hard work. You've sharpened that ax so much. So this is where you talk to people and they'll be like, you can't write a promo in a week. It's kind of true in a way because what they don't know is you've thought about that idea for two months and you've organized that idea. So you absolutely can write an idea in a week. You probably can't conceive of that idea, organize that idea, research that idea, and write that idea in a week. But if you're talking about writing, that's the true part. You're just writing it fast, right? So if you're constantly thinking about all these ideas, you're constantly finding proof. You're constantly thinking about structure. Then when it comes to writing, that's the easy part of writing it in a week. That's the thing that we're able to do in a way that most freelance copywriters would say, that's possible. You can't write a promo in a week. You absolutely can. But it's all this constant idea generation that's happening behind the scenes that they never think about. I'd say you can write it in a day and Jose can test it. I know you guys did it pretty quick in the first ones we did were in a week. But that flew back for a month. We had the idea and sum up the proof. But you can actually, obviously you've got to go up with the idea like you're saying, and then it helps to have all your research. Just like I said, you can brute force your ideas when you're trying to find those ideas. So if you really put your mind to it and you have that structure, you know it's search and destroy. You know exactly what proof you're looking for. So you can just brute force if it's like, how does this work? And you know it's milk thistle. You can just brute force that proof. And just as long as you're disciplined, you can do it. The following piece of point. The inspirational thing to do is what is hard to do consistently. The next point, I mean you can get really lost in research. You can research the craft on every idea forever and ever. Using this, I don't know anything about Jim's idea at all. I'm based on other than what he told me. So the fact that, you know, just spitballing, we come up with all these things. You could find the proof then to fill in all these things. Half of the time. The other thing I think is important too is one of the things we'll be doing for you in a page or copy a day. If you were not doing that or if you're not used to doing that, you gotta start doing it. Because again, he came from the DRQ, he was writing DRs every single day. I came from the DRH for two years straight. Every single day I was writing 1500 words. I know how to write. So if you don't know how to do that and you're new to writing, you should be writing it again. So if you're not doing it up to this point, you start writing it every day. Because otherwise when you get to Hell Week, you're gonna be like, what do I do? Like you're not gonna, like, play things, not gonna come. So I'd get that going. I told you guys at the start of this thing that the specialty that we're gonna be focusing in is the most important skill set that you need to have in direct response. Which is how you write sales copy. But I also told you during this thing that it's designed, if we do this the right way, you're designed to be holistic direct marketers when you come out of this thing that understand how to drive traffic. How to do graphic design better than a lot of quote unquote graphic designers. That you'll understand the numbers. This is falling apart, Jim. This is not a good sign for your promo. You can take a picture as soon as you're done. I don't even wear that one. So what you're gonna see during Copy Camp here is we're beginning to transition to that other stuff. So over the next couple weeks, we're gonna start to transition to the numbers of direct response, graphic design of direct response, dealing with legal and compliance in a way that gets your persuasive promo through legal with the least amount of scars coming out of it. We're beginning to finish up this kind of core specialty which is copywriting. So when we move in the next couple weeks, it's gonna move into the direction. You're gonna notice a shift into the direction of marketing, of direct response and the things that come after you have working ideas and promos. Now that all said, again, this is the most important skill, right? Writing copy is the most important skill. So if you have questions as we go along in the next couple weeks, just feel free to bring them up. You know, that we can talk about them in here, but we're gonna start to shift into when your idea is done, when you've written it, what happens next, who's involved next, show you the process, show you the numbers, show you how to drive traffic. Because again, I think that the people that will succeed in this business, I know, let's say, I don't think because that's weak language, I know that the people who succeed in this business are gonna be people who aren't just copywriters and aren't just marketers. It's gonna be the people that are holistically good at doing this business. Meaning that they know how to write copy, they know how to drive traffic, they understand the importance of traffic, they know how to recognize good graphic design. So this is what this course is gonna shift to next, is showing you how to be that person that's gonna succeed while other people who are only specialized in one area will fail or not succeed in our business. Quick questions on this before we end it though. Any questions? You guys all agree just to try it on your next promo. Show of hands. Everybody buying in? You're gonna try it on your next promo? Again, you try it, it doesn't work, then go back to try it and do it a different way, right? Try it right from a blank screen. It doesn't work for me, I don't think it'll work for you guys, but I want you to try this at least on your next promo. I guarantee that it will be beneficial. Well back, you recommended that we should read the promo out loud to a buddy. But I'd say that this is also helpful to have someone that hasn't heard the idea before to try to come up with the objections. Because after you've done all the research, you're thinking on the right side of the column and not the left. That's a similar thing I was gonna say, is trying to get a bunch of people, I don't know how you guys are gonna organize it, but you don't want to have one or two people doing this. Because structurally what we're trying to do, we're just sending hundreds of thousands of people, we're trying to get all of the top objections from those people in this process. If you have one or two people and they're kind of the same, and they're thinking along the same way, you're not gonna get good objections. I'd limit it to no more than five. But I wouldn't get two or three, personally. So, because otherwise you're gonna get paralysis by analysis, you're gonna get so many objections and then you're like, if one out of the five feels that objection and the other four don't, and you're starting to get included in your objections, that it may not actually be an objection that you have to overcome. Like at some point the group gets too big, right? That's why I was saying that this isn't really ideal. Because the group gets too big and you're writing what's called copy by committee, which is never good. You need to have a tight group. I don't know if it makes a difference, but I always try to at least ask one person, like my mom who has no fucking idea what copy is, or marketing, or anything, and see it. I always seem to get like some good nugget out of someone who knows nothing about what you're talking about and what your business is. Even when you're finished your first draft, like this process goes on. When you go through and re-edit your first draft, which is something you should do, we didn't get a lot into editing, but you should definitely, if you're handing out your first draft, you should never do that to somebody else. There should be your third or fourth draft before you actually show somebody else, and definitely before you turn into Joe. But you should be thinking through again every time you read it. Do I have any other objections that I can think of? And you'll constantly be sharpening it. Sometimes you might go back and figure it out that even though you went through this whole process, that you missed something, well, you still have a chance to fix it. Okay, so on Thursday, we are moving on. Again, we're starting to shift a little bit. We're shifting out of writing the full-length promotion, and we're shifting into all the supplemental material that goes with your promotions and why you should care. So we're going to talk about order forms. We're going to talk about upsell paths. We're assuming, again, that you're moving along with this kind of sales idea. If there's any questions about continued sales process, of course, that's the most important thing we're going to talk about those. But again, you will notice a noticeable shift here. Pete's got something to say. Just on the copy board, I tried to do this presentation in Europe with the farm people, and I came up with another thing that I've been doing personally. Because since Joe's taught me this, I've started to try to reduce the whole world to copy boards. And so when I read a piece of copy each day, like we're supposed to be doing, I try, I'm a little time consuming, but I try to reverse or reward those promos. So literally, I'll mark up the promo like normal and go in the margins and write whatever I think the person was trying to do in each line. But at the end, then, I'll do a one sheet where I go over the author that will film in everything in the one sheet. But at the end of the one sheet, I'll literally re-type all of the subheads that were in that promo, and then I'll try to reduce them to the objections that they address. And then, again, it's just a really cool shortcut. For example, like Kyle, I know you were talking about, like, I want to do a dark DTA promo, that's kind of a version of dark traits. If you reverse storyboard dark traits, then you kind of already have a good sense of what's worked. You can compare whatever storyboard you have to what's working the best. So there are storyboards out there right now that are proven. But you can just really take, and it just takes looking beneath that subhead and realizing it addresses some objection and then reducing it. So you can do this process in the reverse each day you're reading the promos. Absolutely. This is where it helps to save and archive all the promos that you've read in some type of, you know, easily accessible archive, because you can go back and say, this idea is like that one. It's a new idea, but it's like that one in structure. Therefore, I'm going to reverse storyboard and have my storyboard based on that promo that I read before. So you save yourself a bunch of work. Jim? I just want to thank everyone in the room. You made me like a bloody... Okay, we'll see you guys Thursday. Order forms, on time offers, funnels, all that good stuff. You
